data.title: Geron: Underpromise And Overdeliver Could Be The 2026 Strategy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The launch of Geron Corporation's (GERN) Rytelo (imetelstat) for lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes (LR-MDS) has not been a roaring success, but a January 2026 update provides us with some insights on the road ahead. I rated GERN a hold in November 2025, noting the company's launch was stalling out, although the drug had at least grown to nearly $50M in net sales per quarter, and there was potential to grow the drug further. This article looks at the December and January updates from the company to see where GERN stands now.

Rytelo launch recap

Rytelo achieved a fairly fast launch during its first few quarters, but despite repeatedly reporting progress in growing the percentage of patients receiving Rytelo as a first- and second-line therapy, the launch has stalled out.

With Q3'25 earnings, the CEO did not refer to a turnaround in any short time frame, instead referring to the company or its launch as a 2026 growth story.

Faisal Khurshid - Leerink Partners LLC, Research Division

And then, do you have a timeline that you're willing to kind of guide the Street? I don't know when we should expect growth.

Harout Semerjian - CEO, President & Director

No, we're saying at this point, this is a 2026 growth story, Faisal. And we are not giving any guidance at this point on our top line, but we look forward to that being something that we plan to tackle in the near future. So we're assessing at this point.

But we think now we've put our heads down, get to work. And in 2026, this is a growth story over there. But we're not giving any specific guidelines in terms of is it's going to start growing on March 31 or April 15. That's not what we're saying.

GERN Q3'25 earnings call, November 5, 2025. Emphasis mine.

GERN's 2026 guidance

GERN hasn't reported Q4'25 preliminary revenues, like some other biotechs have in January, but the company did put out a press release on January 12, 2026, guiding for net product revenues from Rytelo of $220M-$240M in 2026. On the one hand then, there is still a chance of a revenue surprise later in the quarter when GERN reports Q4'25 earnings; on the other hand, one might wonder if the Q4'25 sales numbers were a little disappointing. Perhaps the company wants to at least be able to tell investors things are looking up in Q1'26, when they report Q4'25 earnings.

Even the top end of GERN's 2026 guidance is $14M below the 2026 analyst estimate for GERN's revenues, which could reflect excessive optimism of those analysts. After all, GERN's past four quarters of revenues don't paint a picture of growth. Still the possibility that the company is now under-promising, consistent with the CEO's statements on the Q3'25 earnings call, is in play.

Beyond GERN's 2026 revenue guidance, the January 12 press release also came with guidance for total operating expenses of $230M-$240M in 2026. That would represent a $20M reduction from GERN's 2025 guidance for total operating expenses. GERN's exact 2025 operating expenses aren't known since the company hasn't reported Q4'25 earnings.

One way of meeting that guidance for reduced expenses in 2026 is the restructuring the company announced in December 2025. GERN noted at that time there would be a ~1/3 reduction in workforce. GERN had $421.5M in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and investments at the end of Q3'25, and although it did have a long-term principal debt balance of $119.3M at the end of Q3'25, that debt doesn't mature until November 2029. Even if GERN can't quite hit its product revenue guidance in 2026, or operating expenses are higher than expected, I can't see the company having to raise near-term. Indeed, net cash used in operating activities was $89M in the first nine months of 2025, and given the company is completing an ~1/3 reduction in workforce, GERN's 2026 guidance for operating expenses doesn't seem aggressive. Indeed, the company may be planning to under-promise and over-deliver on the expense reduction front. GERN trades with a market cap of $880.9M ($1.38 per share) and an enterprise value of ~$580M, albeit using cash and debt at the end of Q3'25. If GERN burned through another $30M in Q4'25, then the current enterprise value might be closer to ~$610M.

Potential Near-term catalysts

GERN does have approval for Rytelo in the EU for the treatment of LR-MDS, and has been considering partners for an EU launch. The details of any such partnership are a key potential catalyst, since any upfront payment could be variable, as could milestones and royalties. GERN's approval from the European Commission was announced in March 2025, so the company has had plenty of time since that approval to discuss the launch with partners.

So those conversations are happening as we speak. But we want to make sure that it's the right people, it's the right timing...

... So stay tuned on that. That's ongoing, and we will update the market as we have new updates.

Harout Semerjian, CEO, President and Director at GERN, Q3'25 earnings call.

Beyond Rytelo in LR-MDS, the company is expecting to reach an interim analysis from its registrational myelofibrosis trial, IMpactMF, in 2026. An early stoppage due to success at that interim would signal a more obvious source of revenue growth for the company, and slight increases or decreases in operating expenses might become less of a focus.

If that interim passes without a stoppage, then a final analysis in 2028 means years more investment into myelofibrosis without a nearer-term launch, if the drug even succeeds at the final analysis.

Risks

There are multiple risks to a long position in GERN, a few of which will be discussed in this section.

Firstly, GERN could report lackluster Q4'25 sales, which could disappoint the market, even if the CEO is not guiding for a short-term turnaround in the Rytelo launch. For example, one could interpret that GERN's restructuring, which came in early December, might have been approved following middling sales in October and perhaps November.

Additionally, GERN could pass the interim analysis in IMpactMF without an early stoppage, which could trigger concerns on whether or not the drug works, and hit home that Rytelo needs to perform in the current indication.

Lastly, GERN could look to do business development beyond Rytelo, such as in-licensing a new drug. Such an action could provide another potential revenue source, but could also raise concerns about the company's confidence in Rytelo, leading to a fall in the stock.

Conclusion

GERN's 2026 guidance for Rytelo product revenue shows that the company does expect revenue growth, despite four quarters of sales that look pretty similar. Perhaps the company has seen something in the last weeks of 2025, or simply has confidence that it can grow Rytelo from its current sideways sales performance. For example, the company did make ground on increasing Rytelo use in first- and second- line LR-MDS, which didn't seem to manifest as increased sales, but perhaps it will help patients stay on therapy longer, which would take longer to reflect in sales. Topline growth seems like a very realistic prospect for GERN, and the mere confirmation of that could justify upside in the stock. For example, GERN currently trades with an EV/Sales of 3.15, just five full quarters into the launch of Rytelo, below the sector median of 3.94 noted on Seeking Alpha's valuation tab.

Further, beyond the indications that there could be some growth ahead for Rytelo (company guidance, the potential for use in earlier lines to start to impact revenues), GERN is serious about reducing expenses. A ~1/3 reduction in workforce is a welcome step that wasn't taken under previous management. GERN's new CEO (Semerjian was appointed CEO and President in August 2025). Semerjian's approach of tempering expectations, by not guiding for a turnaround on a specific date opens up the possibility that the company is planning to under-promise and over-deliver. GERN's 2026 guidance on revenues implying actual growth (rather than the past few middling quarters), the restructuring, and the potential for an early stoppage in IMpactMF (which could trigger a run up in the stock), have me now rating the stock a buy.

data.title: Smurfit Westrock: 3 Reasons To Consider This Underperforming Packaging Specialist
data.description:
Underperforming Since Listing

Smurfit Westrock Plc (SW), an Irish-based business that was created and listed only in July 2024, by way of a strategic combination between the European-based integrated corrugated packaging manufacturer - Smurfit Kappa Group plc, and the North American-based paper packaging solutions expert - Westrock Company, hasn't yet managed to get going since it began life as a public company.

To elaborate, SW which currently produces and sells a range of paper-based packaging (of differing characteristics) in 40 countries around the world (SW has three broad geographical segments- North America, Latin America, and Europe, Middle East, Africa, and Asia Pacific) has seen its stock contract by around 1% since it got listed, while its peers from the S&P 500 and materials space have surged ahead by around 22-27% during the same period.

After 18 months of considerable volatility and no real progress, the fidelity of long-standing investors towards this stock may likely be wearing thin. However, we feel SW is still worth getting behind, more so at this juncture. Here are a few reasons why we believe in the investment case of SW.

The Onset Of A More Profitable And Efficient North American Business

While SW generates business from three broad regions across the world, the bulk of the business (close to 60%) comes from the North American region alone, and thus, developments in this territory are seen as a key driver of sentiment towards this stock.

Passive observers of SW who take a cursory look may be troubled to note that corrugated container volumes from this region have been on a descent for multiple quarters now, with the most recent Q3 witnessing a doubling of the pace of declines to almost -9%.

However, don't fret, as this loss of volume is a very deliberate ploy by the management team to get rid of sub-par business volumes of the legacy business, and pivot rather to higher-quality customer accounts (they refer to this as their "value over volume" strategy). SW's efforts on this front, can be gleaned by how the EBITDA margin profile of the North American segment has continued to improve over time (currently at over 17% levels) even as volumes take a hit.

Investors can also brace themselves to see further improvements on the margin front, as price hikes that have been on hold since late 2024, are due to take effect in H1 this year; note that SW's close peer - Packaging Corporation of America (PKG), is due to increase North American containerboard prices by $70 per ton from March 1st

Crucially, SW's volume contraction isn't expected to go on forever, and management believes that the shedding of underperforming contracts will run out in the current quarter or Q2, with volume growth expected from Q3 onwards.

Investors should also consider that SW is not just right-sizing its commercial profile in North America but also its operating profile, with underperforming and loss-making plants being shut down. SW shut down over 500K tons of capacity both in containerboard and consumer board last year, and this should give a fillip to utilization rates, which should also reflect well on margins. Admittedly, SW isn't the only North American player that has been curtailing production capacity, but the point is, with around 10% of production capacity being taken out of the entire system, the market position should begin to turn more favorably.

A Well-Rounded Dividend Profile

An underappreciated facet of the SW story is also the relatively useful quarterly dividends that this business has been doling out since August 2024. In a few days from now (based on when last year's hike was announced), SW will likely announce its annual hike of its quarterly dividends, given that they subscribe to a " progressive dividend policy".

The last time SW hiked its dividend, it came in at an impressive cadence of 42% YoY, and even if one is unlikely to see a similarly steep hike, there is still reason to appreciate the dividend here.

For a company to raise its dividends every year, it is going to need a very resolute FCF foundation, and that's what you get with SW. The company's operating cash flows are growing at a very impressive pace (for the first 9 months of the year, OCF has more than tripled YoY from $0.7B to $2.2B, with the company on course to deliver over $3B of annualized operating cash flow for the first time).

Yes, SW is also incurring a much higher threshold of CAPEX (as they are looking to enhance the legacy Westrock business), but the YoY growth in FY26 won't be prohibitive. To elaborate, CAPEX, which was previously annualizing to less than $1B p.a. in FY22/FY23, rose to $1.5B p.a. in FY24, and is now around the $2.3B mark. However, guidance for next year points to a 7% YoY hike to around $2.45B.

At $0.43 a share, cash commitments towards dividends currently annualize to around $0.9B p.a., which means SW basically needs to generate at least $3.5 worth of operating cash p.a. to meet both the dividends and CAPEX, which is certainly doable, particularly considering the pricing and EBITDA uplift that will take place this year.

Going forward, there is also ample scope for operating cash flow to step up meaningfully, as management believes they can optimize working capital spend further (working capital as a function of sales is around 16%, and management has described this as "higher than we'd like it to be"). We can see how there's room for improvement on this front, as the cash tied up with working capital has crept up over the years and is now well over two months.

Interestingly, consensus is currently budgeting for an annual dividend figure of $1.78 (or $0.445 a share per quarter) in FY26, which would represent a single digit percentage hike on a YoY basis. Also consider that the cash bill associated with a hike of that magnitude would be perfectly reasonable at less than $0.95B p.a.

Even without a hike, it's worth noting that SW's dividend yield of 3.97% is already better than what other packaging stocks currently offer on average ( less than 3.6%); and at $1.78 a share, one gets to lock in a 4.1% yield at current prices.

To conclude this section, we view SW's dividend profile as quite attractive, as not only do you get a solid yield, but decent growth, as well as comfortable coverage backing it.

Ongoing Momentum and Further Mean Reversion Potential

SW's standalone and relative strength charts support a buy on account of two different reasons.

On the standalone daily chart, note that SW is currently witnessing good momentum, since bottoming out in November 2025. Basically, we are now seeing a series of higher-highs and higher lows, even as the SW continues to trend up using the support of an ascending trendline. Crucially, the price is now also trading above all its key moving averages- 50, 100, and 200, with the break over the latter coming across as quite poignant, considering how the stock had previously failed to break that barrier. Until recently, since March 2025, SW had not been able to trade above and defend its 200DMA. Broadly, this suggests stronger bullish support for the stock.

Meanwhile, if we shift to the relative strength [RS] charts, we can see how SW is positioned versus its peers from the large-cap material space. Note that traditionally, SW's relative strength ratio versus an equally weighted basket of stocks from the materials segment of the S&P 500 has averaged around 0.87x. One could make a case for SW benefitting from some upward mean reversion as its current ratio is around 17% lower than its long-term mean.

Conclusion

Smurfit Westrock, which has not set the world alight since getting listed in July 2024, appeals to us because of steps taken to right-size and enhance its largest regional segment (North America), the presence of a very well-rounded dividend profile founded on improving cash flow dynamics, and ongoing momentum on the charts, with further upward mean reversion potential. To conclude, we rate SW a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Emerging Markets Debt Shows Staying Power
data.description:
Despite heightened concerns around global trade tensions driven by U.S. policy and ongoing geopolitical conflicts, we believe EM fundamentals remain resilient, and we expect this backdrop to continue in 2026.

Specifically, EM growth is projected to be roughly 3.7% on a gross domestic product (GDP)-weighted basis (broadly in line with 2025), supporting stable fiscal positions and debt-to-GDP ratios across most countries. At the same time, benign inflation should allow continued monetary easing, while AI-related productivity gains provide an additional tailwind to growth.

External accounts stand out as a key strength. Persistent capital inflows, robust foreign direct investment, and healthy current account balances underpin solid balance-of-payments positions.

Although U.S. tariffs rose sharply in 2025, their impact proved less severe than initially feared, and EMs are increasingly insulated as intra-EM trade now exceeds half of total trade. This reduced reliance on advanced economies should help EMs weather softer developed-market growth, keeping default risk contained and fundamentals constructive.

Technical conditions remain supportive. We believe net new issuance should stay below historical averages, aided by ongoing bilateral and multilateral funding, while underallocation among global investors leaves room for higher EM exposure in 2026.

Valuations are constructive: hard-currency spreads are tight but all-in yields remain elevated, with scope for further compression - especially in high yield - alongside lower U.S. Treasury yields, supporting benchmark returns.

We continue to see opportunities in select EM corporates and frontier markets. We believe frontier debt is supported by high real yields, improving external balances, and selective room for further rate cuts, while EM corporates may offer spread pickup over sovereigns.

Below, we break down some of our largest active hard currency positions in each beta bucket.

Beta-Bucket Approach: Select Active Positions

To allocate capital and budget risk effectively across a large and diverse set of countries, we use a proprietary grouping framework. Rather than organizing by region, we classify issuers into low-, medium-, and high‑beta buckets. We believe this approach captures true risk profiles given the wide range of development levels within regions and allows for fairer comparisons and more optimal portfolio construction.

Risk budgets are allocated dynamically: When our top‑down scores are more positive, we increase exposure to high‑beta countries. Portfolio‑level exposure is monitored and adjusted continuously to maintain an optimal risk/return balance.

Source: William Blair, as of December 2025. Beta buckets are based on the team’s qualitative and quantitative analysis. Risk buckets are provided for illustrative purposes only and are not intended as investment advice or as projections of future returns. Overweights/underweights may vary between vehicles.

High-Beta Bucket

The largest overweight positions on a spread duration basis were in Argentina, Sri Lanka, and El Salvador. Conversely, the largest underweight positions were in Nigeria, Kenya, and Ivory Coast.

Argentina (overweight): We believe Argentina remains on a positive fundamental trajectory following the midterm results and policy direction, and prefer amortizing bonds, which are shorter duration. We also hold a position in the Province of Buenos Aires, as well as GDP warrants.

Sri Lanka (overweight): Improving GDP growth, lower oil prices, and declining domestic interest rates support our overweight to Sri Lanka’s macro-linked bonds (SRILAN MLBs). While cyclone-related infrastructure damage in the fourth quarter created near-term costs, the stronger foundation built in recent years supports continued pricing of an intermediate upside, with momentum building toward a higher upside as MLB trigger mechanisms come into play.

El Salvador (overweight): El Salvador remains on an improving trajectory, assuming it continues on the policy path agreed upon with the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and we like the shape of its yield curve.

Nigeria (underweight): Valuations are tight and we expect oil price dynamics to be less supportive. While there has been strong monetary policy reform, with the improved functioning of the exchange rate forming a key improvement to the macroeconomic backdrop, the fiscal outlook remains more complex, with low revenue mobilization a key structural weakness.

Kenya (underweight): We believe Kenya is likely to remain an exception to the fiscal consolidation trend in Sub-Saharan Africa. The draft 2026 medium-term budget plan signals continued fiscal pressures and limited scope for aggressive deficit reduction ahead of the 2027 elections, with authorities leaning on public-private partnerships and arrears paydown for stimulus. While growth is resilient, inflation stable, and external buffers adequate, fiscal vulnerabilities and political risks ahead of the 2027 elections argue for a defensive stance.

Ivory Coast (underweight): Fundamentals are strong, and the October 2025 presidential election was orderly, with the incumbent securing a comfortable victory. While domestic banks have some exposure to Senegalese local currency debt, rating agencies view the exposure as relatively small and do not see Senegal’s debt-solvency concerns as a risk to the banking sector. That said, valuations are tight and a restructuring could indirectly impact the region.

Medium-Beta Bucket

The largest overweight positions on a spread duration basis were in Mexico, Brazil, and Trinidad and Tobago. Conversely, the largest underweight positions were in Bahrain, Turkey, and South Africa.

Mexico (overweight): We remain overweight Pemex, as we believe it is well supported by the sovereign and has very attractive valuations versus the sovereign. We are overweight the Mexican sovereign based on valuations and maintain corporation positions.

Brazil (overweight): We have a bias toward corporate positions as we see better opportunities following the strong sovereign performance in the second half of 2025. Current exposure includes oil and gas via an independent producer and an issuer tied to the Petrobras ecosystem, alongside an environmental services issuer.

Trinidad and Tobago (overweight): Despite a declining fundamental trajectory, initial conditions remain strong, as Trinidad and Tobago has significant wealth in their Heritage and Stabilization Fund. Valuations are also appealing relative to peers.

Bahrain (underweight): Regional geopolitical volatility remains elevated, and fiscal-reform momentum has been limited despite persistent structural challenges. Lower oil prices have reduced fiscal flexibility, while valuations remain tight relative to fundamentals, offering little margin for error. With supply still heavy after recent issuance and limited progress on debt sustainability, we see more attractive opportunities elsewhere in the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) universe.

Turkey (underweight): Valuations have tightened significantly, limiting upside on a risk-adjusted basis. While inflation is expected to moderate by the end of 2026 and we believe growth should stabilize around 3%, elevated refinancing costs and high real rates continue to pressure corporate balance sheets. Ongoing policy-credibility concerns and geopolitical risks add uncertainty, reinforcing our cautious stance despite recent improvements in external accounts and reserve buffers.

South Africa (underweight): Persistent fiscal vulnerabilities and structural challenges continue to weigh on our outlook. Despite attractive nominal yields, weak growth prospects, rising debt-service costs, and ongoing state-owned enterprise concerns limit appeal. Political uncertainty ahead of the 2026 local elections and limited progress on fiscal consolidation further dampen sentiment, leaving spreads vulnerable to volatility with little compensation for underlying risks.

Low-Beta Bucket

The largest overweight positions on a spread duration basis were in Paraguay, Oman, and United Arab Emirates. Conversely, the largest underweight positions were in Malaysia, China, and the Philippines.

Paraguay (overweight): Valuations are attractive relative to low-beta peers and fundamentals are solid. The country’s debt-to-GDP ratio remains low, and the country has a strong history of low fiscal deficits. S&P also upgraded Paraguay to investment grade in December 2025.

Oman (overweight): Despite recent oil-price volatility, Oman stands out as a defensive play in the region thanks to its strong commitment to fiscal discipline and debt reduction. Its authorities have demonstrated credible progress on structural reforms, and valuations remain attractive relative to regional peers, reinforcing our constructive stance. Thus, we believe the country’s fundamental credit-improvement story remains firmly intact.

United Arab Emirates (overweight): The UAE continues to benefit from robust economic diversification, strong external buffers, and proactive fiscal management. These factors underpin credit stability and offer resilience against regional volatility. We like corporates and smaller emirates, where valuations are compelling compared to Abu Dhabi.

Malaysia (underweight): Valuations are tight. We hold selective overweight exposure in quasi-sovereign bonds on more attractive valuations relative to the sovereign bonds in the index.

China (underweight): Valuations are tight in sovereign and quasi-sovereign bonds, though we hold selective corporate bonds at attractive valuations and positive credit trajectories.

Philippines (underweight): We have valuation concerns relative to the Philippines’ peers. Macroeconomic fundamentals are broadly stable, but a weaker growth outlook and intermittent geopolitical tensions in the South China Sea with China have weighed on risk sentiment without materially disrupting financial market stability.

Original Post

data.title: FS KKR: Investing In The Bonds Looks Better Than Trading The Bottom Now
data.description:
Contrary to the income nature of business development companies, they are among the most volatile income investments. Sometimes it pays to trade them, but this requires real trading expertise. We rarely switch from a trader to an investor, but in the following lines, we will defend the thesis that a stable bond investment may even outperform the expected return of a company's common stock.

FS KKR Capital (FSK) is among the largest business development companies in the BDC sector. Its current market capitalization is around $4.05 billion, and its total assets at the end of the 3rd quarter are close to $13.09 billion. The company has more than 224 holdings across 23 industries. This does not prevent it from being one of the worst performers in the sector, and its valuation at a close to 35% discount seems more of a concern than an opportunity. We were bottom-picking FSK at some point, and you can see more details in our article on this case here. While this was just a trade for us, the idea we are about to present today is a true "Alpha" based on our research and is hidden in the company's bonds, which we will refer to as the "OTC bonds."

OTC bonds

FSK has 7 over-the-counter bonds:

The highest-yielding instrument in the picture above comes with a yield to maturity of around 6.7%. The bond has approximately 5 years to maturity, with a maturity date of 01/15/31. It was issued on 09/25/25 with a coupon of 6.125%, spread to the treasury yield close to 2.36% at the time. Currently, this spread is around 2.96%, and we see no major event that would make this spread widen.

Below, you can see almost all the BDC bonds, and when sorted by ask yield, FSK appears among the highest-yielding BDCs. Add to this its investment-grade rating from Moody's, and we start wondering whether this is an opportunity to gain extra yield without taking on extra risk.

We do our own credit scoring for BDCs, and the closest comparison in credit metrics comes from CGBD and GSBD, which are very similar:

One would expect similar yields:

Compared to GSBD, FSK bonds have a yield above 1%, while the spread to CGBD bonds is slightly lower at around 0.6%. It is as simple as that: similar credit risk, higher yield. To gain greater confidence in our purchase of FSK's bonds, we need to further examine the company's credibility.

Credit metrics

  • Profitability: Here, we get a 3-year average Net Income/Assets of around 3.56%.
  • Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio): The regulatory minimum for BDCs is 150%. FSK's asset coverage ratio cushion is close to 25.83%, with an asset coverage ratio of close to 189%.
  • Asset Quality- The company has 2.9% non-accrual loans and around 18% secured debt. Close to 75% of the assets are senior-secured. Around 87% of debt investments are floating rate.
  • Debt Structure: Being among the largest BDCs comes with nicely spread maturities that are easy to refinance:

  • Weighted Average Yield on Accruing Debt Investments: 10.5%, which is pretty standard for the sector
  • Median portfolio company EBITDA & leverage: close to $115 million/5.9x. - We post this metric as a reminder that FSK's portfolio is quite normal compared to the BDC sector, while its bond yields are an outlier.

Given these data points, FSK is rated as one of the primary BDC issuers. In our methodology, FSK gets punished based on its widened discount to NAV. The market-adjusted coverage ratios and expected profitability lower the credit score, but not to the point of being out of the investment-grade category. While all credit problems will be absorbed by holders of the equity, bondholders can view this company as business as usual.

The Common Or The Bonds?

If we compare the FSK'31 bond with the FSK's common stock using historical data to determine the future discount-adjusted ROE of FSK and leverage the bond in a similar manner, we get these numbers :

The average asset yield of the common stock is around 11.3%, the average loss of yield is close to 2.67%, and the expected ROE for the common stock is 4.97%, but since the common is at a discount of close to 34%, the ROE increases to 7.55%. These values ​​are derived from historical data over the past 4 years. Then, we add leverage (from IBKR) to the OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. Under these conditions, the expected average ROE is close to 9.02%. FSK's bond has a higher ROE and much lower risk.

Bottom line

FSK is a BDC trading at a discount of more than 30%. Investors seem to see its future performance as challenging and punish its valuation by widening its discount. While we see the logic in this behavior, given the low expected ROE even at this discount, we fail to understand why, all of a sudden, the company's bonds are treated among the worst in the sector. To us, this presents an alpha opportunity to invest at a close to 7% yield in an entity with regulatory-restricted leverage that manages to post positive financial results, with low duration and credit risk.

data.title: Veolia Isn't Exciting - That's Exactly The Point
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

This company quietly keeps the essentials moving.

It controls water, waste, and energy, the type of necessary services everybody takes for granted until something goes wrong.

The company is none other than Veolia Environnement SA (VEOEY). The business model of Veolia is straightforward yet strong. It enters into long-term contracts with cities and industries to provide them with services they cannot work without.

And that is what makes Veolia so special. Such contracts last for years, are indexed to inflation, and lie at the core of public infrastructures. This creates a recurring and stable revenue that is hard to disrupt.

A case in point is the 9M FY2025 results, which tell that this is not merely a theory. However, the market is still not convinced. Veolia continues to trade below the sector median even after the predictable cash flows and better fundamentals.

In essence, investors are valuing an infrastructure-supported durable business as one that has an uncertain growth. That disconnect matters.

All in all, VEOEY’s story is not based on hype but on contracts, discipline, and time. Therefore, I rate Veolia as a 'strong buy' at this point.

Strong Operations Power Growth

Veolia keeps demonstrating why its multi-utilities model is so successful.

In 2025, the first nine months’ revenue hit €32.3 billion, a 3.2% growth without including the energy prices. Such expansion reflects the power of long-term municipal and industrial contracts, which are 11 years on average. More than 70% of revenues are inflation-indexed. Hence, Veolia is naturally protected against economic cycles and political fluctuations.

To top that, 80% of income is generated from foreign markets, which provide greater growth and superior margins. Operational performance is also outstanding. EBITDA increased 5.4% to €5.08 billion, improving the margin to 15.7%. Current EBIT rose 7.9% to €2.737 billion.

In addition, Suez merger synergies resulted in efficiency gains of €295 million and €73 million, representing disciplined execution. Retaining 38% of efficiency gains at the EBITDA level is also impressive as it enhances profitability in a significant manner.

Considering the business lines, water operations are the core of Veolia. They created €9.43 billion, up 3.9% due to high volumes and rising prices in Spain, Central Europe, and the U.S. Meanwhile, Water Technologies, the high-margin booster segment, contributed €3.6 billion and provided 10% organic EBITDA growth. Indeed, investment in innovation is yielding results. Solid Waste remained steady at €8.397 billion, which shows the stability of Veolia's operations in Europe.

Hazardous Waste also increased 5.5%, including tuck-in acquisitions, as the high U.S. incineration volumes contributed to the growth. Energy services such as Bioenergy, Flexibility, and Energy Efficiency had an 8.2% organic increase, helped by both acquisitions and increased demand in Europe and the Middle East.

Strength in Smart Decisions

The success of Veolia's operations is backed by its financial management.

Net debt clocked in at €19.9 billion. Yes, it is slightly higher than the previous year, but that’s due to €2.3 billion in targeted investments, which includes €1.5 billion of position in Water Technologies. Nonetheless, leverage is comfortably below 3x, and 93% of debt is fixed-rate.

This structure, together with a seamless refinancing timeline and implementation of hybrid green bonds, provides Veolia with the flexibility to invest without compromising investment-grade ratings.

Furthermore, capital allocation tells a consistent story. In 2024, Veolia liquidated around €1 billion of non-strategic assets and reinvested that amount in better-paying growth areas, like Water Technologies and Hazardous Waste. The Suez merger is still providing real value. Combined with organic growth, these gains contributed to raising current net income by about 9%.

Looking ahead, the GreenUp targets of management remain solid. EBITDA is projected to increase to €8 billion in 2027, and net income is projected to grow at a 10% compound rate. Dividends are estimated to increase in line with earnings.

Well, one might think that this is aggressive forecasting, but it’s not. It shows a balancing strategy of stability and selective growth, making sure every euro spent would enhance the profitability and long-term positioning.

Clean Earth Acquisition Boosts U.S. Growth

The Clean Earth acquisition will be a decisive move towards the growth of the Veolia Hazardous Waste platform, and to be honest, the timing cannot be any better.

Demand for hazardous management is intensifying. It is due to the emergence of new pollutants, a clean-energy surge, and modernized industries like semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and healthcare. Clean Earth has 82 specialized sites with more than 700 permits that complement the current infrastructure of Veolia, providing the firm with scale and agility. This action also fits well with the GreenUp strategy, solidifying high-growth booster segments that are likely to provide strong returns over the coming years.

The deal is financially disciplined and sound. The enterprise value is $3 billion, or approximately 9.8x estimated 2026 EBITDA, and run-rate synergies of $120 million till Year 4. These synergies are realistic, and current EPS is projected to increase as early as Year 2 with Veolia's proven track record in integrating acquisitions. The operation is financed by available funds and debt, with more than €2 billion in asset disposals, maintaining a healthy balance sheet along with growth.

More than the statistics, this acquisition is about developing a platform for long-term value. It will enhance Veolia's U.S. presence, make it stronger in a rapidly expanding market, and enable the company to present a wide range of high-value solutions nationwide.

Valuation and Price Point Analysis

Veolia does not have to surprise to perform. It just requires time and a market that is ready to see what already exists.

The one-year snapshot above already tells us that the market is shifting its mind. The shares of Veolia saw a 26% upswing over the last year, but the journey counts more than the destination.

The stock spent the majority of the year in a small range following an initial rally. Such consolidation following an upward move normally indicates accumulation rather than exhaustion. Sellers were unable to drive the stock into a prolonged downtrend, while buyers constantly repelled pullbacks.

Later in the year, higher lows started taking shape, and the stock went closer to its 52-week high. This trend basically means that the market is absorbing supply and preparing for continuation as opposed to reversal.

The technical arrangement is more compelling when forward earnings anticipations are assessed. Analysts now predict Veolia to produce $1.69 EPS in 2025 and $1.83 in 2026. At its current share price of nearly $18, that puts the stock at a forward P/E of 10.6x for 2025 and below 10x for 2026.

In a regulated, infrastructure-intensive business with recurrent revenue and high earnings visibility, such multiples appear to be squashed. The market, in effect, is valuing Veolia as though earnings growth is weak despite estimates suggesting consistent improvement.

Revenue forecasts support that perspective. The sales are projected to increase from about $53.7 billion in 2025 to roughly $55.7 billion in 2026. Combined with low forward valuation multiples, it establishes an apparent mismatch between risk perception and underlying fundamentals.

The relative valuation enhances the argument even more. Forward EV to EBITDA is at approximately 6.4x against a sector average of 12x. While forward EV to sales is closer to 1x versus the sector median of over 4x. A PEG ratio close to one indicates that investors are paying little to see growth that is already evident in forecasts. In the meantime, a dividend yield of over 4% offers tangible downside protection as investors await a normalization of valuation.

The case base is simple from here. Assuming Veolia just generates the projected EPS of $1.83 in 2026 and then trades at a conservative 11x earnings, the stock would be worth about $20 to $21. The situation presupposes no multiple expansion, no optimism, and no sentiment improvement. It shows stable execution and a cautious market. Even in that scenario, investors will have mid-teens total return when including dividends.

The bull case will need only a slight re-rating. With earnings of $1.83, and the market using a still-reasonable 14x-15x multiple, far less than the sector median, the stock would be trading in the $26-$28 range. The confidence in the sustainability of cash flows, further growth of dividends, and a reduced valuation gap relative to the sector would lead to that result.

Well, it is not a blue-sky situation. It merely supposes that Veolia is slowly appreciated as the defensive utility that it already is.

Risks to My Thesis

Risks are always present, even with a business as stable as Veolia.

The first risk that I consider is regulation. Veolia collaborates closely with governments and municipalities, which is excellent in terms of stability but not necessarily in terms of flexibility. Regulators may pull back on the pricing, contract terms, or indexation when the budget gets narrowed or politics get noisy. Once the inflation protection gets weak or the contracts are renegotiated, margins may tighten faster than anticipated.

Next is acquisition execution, particularly with Clean Earth. I like the deal as it suits the business and enhances the US presence. Nevertheless, integrations are seldom smooth on day one. Systems, people, and synergies take time, and in case of a cost increase or schedule slip, the promised earnings boost might arrive later than anticipated. Veolia has already done this, but scale always increases the stakes.

Then there is the capital-intensive nature of the business. Veolia continuously reinvests in infrastructure, compliance, and modernization. At the moment, the balance sheet is fine. Nonetheless, if interest rates remain elevated over a more prolonged period or the capex requirements increase unpredictably, there might be reduced financial flexibility. That would not break the story but would slow growth in dividends or constrain future deals.

Lastly, the stock can remain undervalued because investors seek higher returns in sectors such as technology, AI, or high-growth energy names. In that situation, the returns would be based less on rapid multiple expansion and more on patience and dividends.

Generally, these risks are controllable. They are just a reminder that strong businesses still need stable and timely execution.

Takeaway

In case you have read this far, the message is not that complicated. Veolia is not a story stock, nor does it need to be. It is built on long-term agreements, essential infrastructure, and disciplined implementation, steadily compounding value as the market pursues louder concepts. The basics are strong, the company continues to deliver, and the valuation provides a margin of safety rare in this sector. You're paid to wait through a sound dividend as earnings quietly continue their work. This will not provide instant entertainment, and that is alright. When you are interested in being patient, lasting, and enjoying a good night's sleep, then this is the type of stock you buy, hold, and let time work in your favor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RZV: Soft Quality, Lackluster Growth Metrics To Detract From Returns
data.description:
It is my expectation that the Invesco S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Value ETF (RZV), a passively managed exchange-traded fund with a maximalist value strategy, will underperform the market proxied with the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) in the short to medium term, so I find it necessary to reiterate the Hold rating I assigned to it in February 2023 and then maintained in January 2024.

For clarity, the main drivers behind my stance are unchanged, namely RZV's small exposure to the quality factor (the low-to-mid single-digit capital efficiency metrics look quite depressing, to be honest), as well as its rather lethargic growth characteristics (a point even could be made that they are simply non-existent, since, as per my calculations, more than half of the holdings are forecast to deliver EPS contraction going forward). So the problem is that in the current market environment, growth and quality are the two main factors to favor, and RZV cannot offer adequate exposure to either of them.

My belief also remains that for maximalist value investors who are tolerant to negative weighted average growth rates and quite compressed margins and just want to maximize their exposure to the value factor, RZV offers access to multiple gems. Of course, they should understand the depth of risks they are taking in this pursuit of distressed valuations, more likely with little or even no reward.

Also, it is necessary to explain why I will not be advocating for a bearish view. The gist is that to construct such a thesis, we would need a catalyst, potentially with sweeping consequences that would reverberate both through the SMID equity universe and the market overall, and I see no such factor at this juncture.

RZV Strategy And Portfolio

What is inside the RZV portfolio at the moment? As of January 23, it encompassed 162 common stocks and REITs, with the top one being SunCoke Energy, Inc. (SXC), a materials company with a 1.34% weight. SXC has an earnings yield of around 9.4% and an A+ Quant Valuation grade, plus it has gained about 12.2% since the beginning of the year (as of writing this article), so it is hardly a coincidence that it tops the list of a pure value ETF's holdings.

A minor remark I should make here is that I noticed a slight recent adjustment to the portfolio: the dataset I downloaded from the Invesco website with holdings as of January 22 showed Guess?, Inc. (traded with a ticker GES) as one of the components. However, since Guess? was "de-listed with its planned sale to Authentic Brands" and deleted from the S&P 600 and RZV's underlying index, it was also removed from the RZV portfolio. So we no longer see it in the version as of January 23 (the most recent one as of the time of writing).

RZV Value Exposure: Impressive With No Doubt

As a reminder, the core idea of the S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Value Index that RZV tracks (as we know from the Invesco website) is not just to offer exposure to relatively inexpensive small-cap names. The screening process is much more stringent here, so no overlap is allowed between the index and its antipode, the S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Growth Index.

This is in contrast with a more relaxed approach of the S&P SmallCap 600 Value Index that does allow the overlap with the S&P SmallCap 600 Growth Index, so this is not a surprise that the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Value ETF (IJS) and the iShares S&P Small-Cap 600 Growth ETF (IJT) share a few holdings.

And RZV's unwillingness to compromise on the value front, as I have already illustrated in the previous articles, does bear fruit, as shown by the metrics below, juxtaposed to those of the iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF, with its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade of B- or higher being especially telling.

And the P/S ratio of just 0.65 is quite impressive too (as the level below 1 is sometimes considered a deep value territory), to be honest.

Importantly, investors should remember that such a large value factor exposure is inextricably intertwined with its sector mix that is light in high-priced IT and communication.

Growth And Quality: Multiple Issues

However, as I usually explain in my ETF analysis pieces, certain sacrifices must be made when calibrating a portfolio's factor mix to someone's investment outlook. And when value exposure is the one to boost, growth is nearly always the front where at least something must be trimmed.

And RZV's weighted average growth rates are not just anemic. The fact is that the EPS and EBITDA growth rates are even negative. More specifically, my analysis shows that 53.6% of the companies in the RZV portfolio are forecast to experience EPS decline and 50.9% are forecast to have lower EBITDA going forward.

Another factor that RZV does not deliver much on is quality.

Even by the small-cap echelon standards, RZV's capital efficiency indicators are depressed, let alone compared to those of IVV. Besides, just about 23% of its holdings have a Quant Profitability grade of B- or higher. The situation is a little brighter in terms of exposure to stocks with attractive Quant EPS Revisions grades but not much.

In sum, this is the very core of my skeptical stance: a portfolio with a 1.4% weighted average forward revenue growth rate and a less than 4% Return on Total Capital is not a place to be in 2026.

RZV Performance Discussion

I would not say that RZV's returns have always been totally lackluster. The fact is that it had a few brilliant years, including 2013, 2016, 2021, etc.

Year RZV's Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
2009 36.06%
2010 13.46%
2012 5.37%
2013 12.97%
2016 21.79%
2021 17.21%
2022 11.35%

Calculated by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

However, its annualized return for the April 2006–December 2025 period was nonetheless 1.2x weaker than IJR's and 1.6x weaker than IVV's.

Other issues that make RZV a rather questionable investment are its downside capture of close to 150% (with IVV used as a benchmark) and a maximum drawdown of 72.27%.

Moreover, my calculations show that its median monthly active return over the period was at -0.10% with IJR used as a benchmark and -0.49% when benchmarked against IVV. And these figures are obviously hardly appealing.

On a side note, please take notice that the data above includes returns delivered by the Guggenheim S&P SmallCap 600® Pure Value ETF prior to the fund's reorganization in April 2018; more details on that can be found in the prospectus.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, even though RZV has had quite a racy start to 2026, which I in part attribute to the Greenland situation and the Japanese bond market crash that gave some impetus to a brief value rotation, I remain neutral on this ETF.

I have not noticed and do not anticipate any structural shifts in the narrative that would be supportive of RZV gaining an edge over IJR or IVV.

This means my neutral stance does not see any changes today. I still believe this vehicle comes with too many risks, chiefly related to its lackluster growth and quality characteristics.

I must admit, though, that a significant change in sentiment (i.e., driving a broad capital rotation situation from the AI economy names to cyclicals, potentially provoked by some disappointing news on AI capex or inflation) might result in a 2021 moment for RZV. Nevertheless, I do not expect it to materialize anytime soon.

data.title: PayPal: A Brutal Competitive Landscape And Changes Yet To Be Seen
data.description:
Introduction

PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) has been a source of debate for some time now, being that it basically finds new lows every month. The reality is that the financial/payment industry has changed significantly, and PayPal was too focused on becoming a super app. As PayPal's past CEO, Dan Schulman said: “Consumers could come to the PayPal app for more than just paying for things online. It could be their one-stop shop for all things shopping and finance”.

During the COVID days of 2021, the market expected that the rapid shift toward e-commerce, digital checkout, and P2P payments would directly benefit PayPal. That expectation, combined with optimism around the 'super-app' narrative, pushed PYPL to a peak price of nearly $300 in July of 2021. At the time, is appeared logical for PayPal to pursue a super-app strategy encompassing multiple payment use cases, such as Braintree for unbranded online purchases, Venmo for P2P social payments, PayPal-branded wallet for consumer payments, Xoom and PayPal World for international transfers/ remittances, Zettle for POS software and hardware such as terminals, Hyperwallet for payouts, PayPal's own USD-pegged stablecoin called PYUSD and a few other tools and services. PayPal learned the hard way that consumer preferences for individuality, concerns around personal data usage, and regulatory constraints make a super-app practically impossible in the West. PayPal, being too focused on its super-app, was not able to properly adapt to the more complex payment/transaction world. In this article, I will explain how fragmented this market is and why I think it is better to wait and see what happens.

Services PayPal Offers

I will start with its fastest-growing service, Venmo, which, after massive user growth, is now trying to monetize its services. One important disruptor that analysts should be worried about is Zelle, an app owned by Early Warning Services, LLC, a firm jointly operated by several large banks. Zelle was initially developed by major US banks such as JPMorgan Chase & Co (JPM), Bank of America Corporation (BAC) and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC), and now it is available through most financial institutions. I think it is more convenient to pay with Zelle because it is directly tied to the user's bank account instead of having to transfer money from the user's bank account to Venmo. When I lived in the U.S., I never opened a Venmo account and did all of the P2P transactions with Zelle, everybody with a bank account already had Zelle so I found no use for it. This is a massive risk as people are already using both and it's just a matter of time for users to adopt the more practical one. Zelle already has more users than Venmo at 151 million accounts compared with to Venmo's 90-95 million. Zelle remains the largest of all P2P apps with a market share of 54%, and its transaction value has already surpassed $1 trillion in 2024. Zelle is not the only threat, as there are many other alternatives, such as Apple Cash, which allows P2P through iMessage, Cash App, and WhatsApp Pay, which is still in the works for many countries.

Xoom is a platform owned by PayPal used for international money transfers and remittances. In this area there are also many alternatives, such as Wise, Revolut, Remitly, Halo, Felix, to name a few. Reading reviews about Xoom leads me to believe that it is one of the worst services in the market as it is “rarely the cheapest option, has a limited number of origin countries, and the exchange rate margin is not disclosed, and 74% of fees are hidden.”

Monito.com also reviewed PayPal in 2023 and classified it as a bad money transfer service (5.9/10) as it offers one of the worst fees and exchange rates on the market. Negative reviews explaining "extremely poor customer service" is a common occurrence on trustpilot.com, and it has a rating of 1.3 stars out of 5.

PayPal World was recently announced, and it allows users to pay in other countries, which management says will unlock over 2 billion new users. It sounds promising but who am I to say it will or will not work? Only time will tell.

There is also fierce competition in the POS software and hardware. PayPal POS, or Zettle, offers a wide range of POS services. It includes a web dashboard for catalog, inventory, reporting, staff, etc., and hardware like terminals and accessories such as printers, docks, barcode scanners, cash drawers, etc. Ecosystems like Square by Block, Inc. (XYZ), SumUp, Clover, Shopify, Inc.'s (SHOP) POS, Toast, Inc. (TOST), and Stripe are competitors in this area although some are more specific to certain workflows. According to 6sense, PayPal ranks number 82 in market share for POS system technologies with approximately 0.02%. It is insignificant in this space.

In the mass-payout platform area where Hyperwallet is there is also competition, primarily with Stripe Connect, Adyen N.V. (ADYEY) and Payoneer Global Inc. (PAYO). In the unbranded checkout space, Braintree competes with Stripe and Adyen with both having a reputation for developer-friendly platforms and direct bank partnerships. Other platforms are Worldpay, Checkout.com, BlueSnap, and a couple of others.

I explain all of this to give you an idea of what PayPal is having to compete with. I think it is impossible and not my job (even as an analyst) to determine which of the services will end up winning in each of the areas that I discussed. From all of this mess of an industry, I can somewhat conclude that PayPal is basically trying to create an ecosystem for the 438 million active accounts that they have when, in reality, it is customary to use many services for different kinds of payments. This is clearly not working, as transactions per active account keep declining. Its services have the highest fees and bad reviews, and it is now easier than ever to switch; this can easily become a downward spiral.

Opportunities

I just talked about the services that PayPal is already in, but now I will discuss its growth opportunities that analysts talk about. PayPal does have the massive data advantage from its 438 million active accounts, but PayPal classifies “active accounts” as accounts that have completed a transaction within the past 12 months, with transactions per active account declining, less data is being collected. Although it has a large number of active accounts, its growth has stalled over the past 3 years growing at around 1-2% annually. I am not too interested in determining if a company’s growth drivers will drive growth; rather, I am more interested once there is evidence that it has worked, even if it involves paying more. Because users use many different services, data for ads is not exclusive to PayPal, so I do not expect it to be the most important player here.

I reviewed PayPal once again when it announced its partnership with Google to create agentic commerce services. Google’s “buy on Gemini” Checkout is built around Google Pay using saved payment/shipping info and it is now expanding into many other methods.

Once again, it is one of many doing these kinds of partnerships, like OpenAI creating an instant checkout powered by the Agentic Commerce Protocol built with Stripe. We also have to see how this will benefit PayPal in the long term, but with so many methods of checking out with LLMs, users will likely use multiple at the same time.

Another big opportunity I see is its massive share buyback program. With its large amount of free cash flow, PayPal has $15 billion authorized for repurchase. I recommend reading Tunga Capital’s Seeking Alpha report explaining PayPal's share buyback program. They calculated that PayPal could reduce its shares by 8.8% every year, as long as PayPal grows modestly. This is the main issue that I see with this buyback program; it only works if PayPal does not release a disappointing quarter.

Valuation

I did a quick DCF, partially because PayPal does not disclose segment earnings. As said before, there is no evidence for a turnaround, so I do not assume one. In fact, I'm leaning more towards further decline in its core services.

Even with pessimistic numbers, PayPal's implied price is still 18% higher. This shows that there is limited downside to this stock, as it is already very cheap. Although the DCF results show a clear margin of safety, I still believe that the high uncertainty from the saturated market diminishes this margin of safety.

Conclusion

So what does this mean for PayPal? PayPal has fierce competition everywhere it looks, and it has not proven that it offers the best service/product. I don't expect this industry to consolidate anytime soon, and right now it looks like PayPal is involved in everything and nothing at the same time. I am not interested in getting involved in a mess of an industry and I am less interested in determining who will win. PayPal does have interesting opportunities but are yet unproven. With so many moving parts, I am not smart enough to determine which opportunities will work. I prefer waiting to see how all of this mess unfolds. I do like the fact that this resembles a call-style bet with limited downside, also that a large part of management is new and the large amount of active accounts it has. I will remain cautious for any proven changes, but right now I give PayPal a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: I Was Bullish At $89, But At $400, It's Time To Sell Before A Potential Bust Cycle
data.description:
In August 2024, I wrote a bullish article about Micron (MU) suggesting it was undervalued. At that time, Micron shares traded for about $89, but the stock has recently experienced a powerful rally and now trades for about $400 per share. Since my article was published, this stock has provided total returns of about 350%, which beats the S&P 500 Index's (SPY) roughly 33% total returns in the same timeframe by nearly a 10x factor. This rally is fueled by unprecedented demand for memory chips used in AI data centers and other applications.

However, this has also led to an unprecedented rally in memory stocks like Micron, which looks like a bubble and a blow-off top to me. I wrote this article when I saw what looked like a blow off top in Netflix (NFLX) in July 2025, and since then the stock has plunged about 35%. This shows it can pay off to sell when investor sentiment appears too optimistic and valuation appears rich. It seems some investors believe this time is different, forgetting that the chip sector, especially memory, is highly cyclical. I remember how Micron shares struggled to get over $100 per share back when I wrote my last article and now it seems like it is one of the most popular momentum stocks in the market. With all of this in mind, let's take a closer look at Micron and why this is possibly a great opportunity to cash in, before a potential bubble pops in the memory chip stocks:

The Charts

To gain maximum perspective on Micron and the chip sector rally, I think it makes sense to look at the 3 charts below. The first chart shows a one-year view for Micron; the stock traded around $60 during the April 2025 lows and now trades for about $400 per share. This stock appears overbought, trading well above the 50-day moving average (around $275) and the 200-day moving average (just about $165).

The chart below shows how Micron shares have traded over the past couple of decades. I think this longer-term view is important for investors to consider now because it shows how unprecedented this rally is when looking at it from a historical perspective. I believe there are two ways to look at this: Bulls could say that this time is different and that AI data center demand has changed everything for Micron and other memory chip companies. However, the bears could argue that even when considering the current demand imbalance for memory, there is no sustainable or fundamental rationale for Micron to trade at its recent valuation.

The chart of the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) below shows that the entire chip sector has rallied over the past year. It also shows that this index has roughly doubled from the lows it hit in April 2025. I think this chart shows that the overall backdrop for the chip sector has been very bullish, likely creating an ideal atmosphere for chip stocks. However, this also raises my concerns that investor sentiment for this sector has become overly bullish.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The earnings estimates below are provided by Seeking Alpha, and show that while Micron earnings and revenues are expected to surge in 2026 and 2027, the growth stalls out and earnings are expected to start dropping in 2028 and beyond as supply constraints likely ease and push down profit margins significantly. If you look deeper into the earnings estimates, you can see that some analysts expect earnings to plunge and nearly halve in 2029, with estimates for that year at just around $19.61 per share. This means that the PE ratio, which looks cheap now, could surge to around 20 times earnings in 2029, for a company that may no longer show revenue or earnings growth after 2029.

Earnings Estimates

FY

EPS

YoY

PE

Sales

YoY
2026

33.17

+300.08%

11.73

$74.75B

+99.98%
2027

42.36
